━━━ WHY AI ETHICS MATTERS ━━━

BACKDROP — Documented AI Harms:
• Amazon's hiring AI (2018): trained on historical data where most hires were male → systematically downgraded women's CVs. Scrapped after internal discovery.
• COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm (US courts): Black defendants twice as likely to be incorrectly flagged as "high risk" of reoffending compared to white defendants with same criminal history.
• Facebook News Feed algorithm (2016-2021): Internal documents show engineers knew it amplified outrage and divisive content — drove engagement metrics but contributed to political polarisation.
• Clearview AI: scraped 3B+ photos from social media without consent for police facial recognition — faces matched to private citizens without due process.
• GPT hallucinations in legal briefs: Two lawyers cited ChatGPT-generated fictional case citations in US federal court (2023). Sanctioned $5,000 each.

━━━ CORE ETHICAL PRINCIPLES ━━━

FAIRNESS:
  Individual fairness: similar people should receive similar predictions.
  Group fairness: accuracy and error rates should be similar across demographic groups.
  Impossibility theorem (2016): several definitions of fairness are mathematically incompatible — you cannot optimise for all simultaneously.

BIAS TYPES IN ML:
  Historical bias:    training data reflects past discrimination
  Representation bias: certain groups underrepresented in training data
  Measurement bias:   proxy features encode protected attributes (zip code ≈ race)
  Aggregation bias:   one model for different groups behaves unequally

  from aif360.sklearn.metrics import equal_opportunity_difference
  # Measures gap in recall between demographic groups
  # Goal: |recall_group_A - recall_group_B| < 0.05

TRANSPARENCY & EXPLAINABILITY:
  "Right to explanation" in GDPR: citizens have the right to an explanation of automated decisions affecting them.
  SHAP values: explain individual predictions from black-box models.
  LIME: local approximation — explains any model's prediction for a specific instance.

━━━ PRIVACY ━━━

Differential Privacy: add calibrated noise to model outputs/training so individual data points cannot be inferred.
  Used by: Apple (iOS keyboard, health data), Google (Chrome usage statistics), US Census Bureau.

Federated Learning: train model on device (phone), only send gradients to server — raw data never leaves the device.
  Used by: Google Keyboard (Gboard), Apple Siri, healthcare institutions sharing models without sharing patient data.

Data minimisation: collect only what is needed. Anonymise before use. Delete when no longer needed.

━━━ SAFETY & ALIGNMENT ━━━

AI Alignment: ensuring AI systems pursue goals their designers intended.
• Specification problem: it's very hard to specify exactly what you want (Goodhart's Law: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure").
• RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — train AI to follow human preferences. Used in ChatGPT, Claude.
• Constitutional AI (Anthropic): model trained to follow a set of ethical principles, critiques own outputs.

━━━ REGULATIONS ━━━

EU AI Act (2024): first comprehensive AI law.
  High-risk AI (credit scoring, hiring, medical, criminal justice) requires:
  • Human oversight
  • Transparency to affected persons
  • Bias audits
  • Registration in EU database
  Prohibited: social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces.

GDPR (2018): right to explanation of automated decisions affecting EU citizens.

US Executive Order on AI (2023): safety testing requirements for large frontier models.

━━━ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES ━━━

• DeepMind AlphaFold: ethically positive — solved protein folding, democratised drug discovery, all results published free.
• Microsoft DALL-E 3: refuses to generate images of real politicians or copyrighted characters — built-in ethical guardrails.
• Apple Face ID: differential privacy on face data — biometric template never leaves device.
• Healthcare AI (IDx-DR): FDA-approved AI for diabetic retinopathy detection. Requires documented bias testing across demographic groups before approval.

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the difference between accuracy parity and equal opportunity?
A: Accuracy parity: model has same accuracy across groups. Equal opportunity: model has same true positive rate (recall) across groups. These can conflict — optimising one may reduce the other. Which matters depends on domain (in medical screening, recall parity is critical).

Q: What is differential privacy and how does it protect individuals?
A: Adds calibrated random noise to computations so an individual's data has negligible impact on the output. Guarantee: output would be nearly identical whether or not you included any one person's data. Prevents membership inference attacks (can't determine if person X was in training set).

Q: Why can't you just "remove" sensitive attributes (race, gender) from the model to make it fair?
A: Other features act as proxies (zip code ≈ race, name ≈ gender). The model learns to reconstruct protected attributes from proxies. Removing the direct attribute while keeping correlated proxies doesn't remove bias — and may hide it.

━━━ WHY IS TIME SERIES SPECIAL? ━━━

BACKDROP:
Standard ML assumes data points are IID (independent and identically distributed) — each row is independent.
Time series data violates this: today's stock price depends on yesterday's. Tomorrow's temperature depends on today's.
Time ORDER matters. You cannot shuffle rows. Future data must never appear in training.
Traditional statistics (ARIMA, 1970s) was purpose-built for this. Neural approaches emerged 2014+.

━━━ COMPONENTS OF TIME SERIES ━━━

A time series = Trend + Seasonality + Residual (noise)
  Trend:       long-term increase/decrease (growing sales, climate warming)
  Seasonality: recurring patterns (holiday spikes, winter heating)
  Residual:    random fluctuations

  from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose
  result = seasonal_decompose(df["sales"], model="multiplicative", period=12)
  result.plot()

━━━ CLASSICAL METHODS ━━━

ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average):
  AR(p): regression on p past values
  I(d):  differencing d times to make series stationary
  MA(q): regression on q past errors

  from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
  model = ARIMA(series, order=(2, 1, 2))
  results = model.fit()
  forecast = results.forecast(steps=30)

PROPHET (Facebook/Meta, 2017):
  Handles trend breaks, holidays, missing data automatically.
  Excellent for business time series with clear seasonality.

  from prophet import Prophet
  model = Prophet(yearly_seasonality=True, weekly_seasonality=True)
  model.fit(df)   # df must have "ds" (date) and "y" (value) columns
  future = model.make_future_dataframe(periods=90)
  forecast = model.predict(future)

━━━ DEEP LEARNING APPROACHES ━━━

LSTM for time series:
  Create windows: use last 30 days to predict next 7.
  Input shape: (batch, sequence_length=30, features)

  N-BEATS (2019): Pure neural architecture, no recurrence — state of the art for univariate.
  Temporal Fusion Transformer (2020): Best for multi-variable time series with known future inputs.

━━━ GRADIENT BOOSTING FOR TIME SERIES ━━━

Transform time series into supervised learning: create lag features.
  df["lag_1"] = df["sales"].shift(1)    # yesterday's sales
  df["lag_7"] = df["sales"].shift(7)    # last week
  df["rolling_mean_30"] = df["sales"].rolling(30).mean()
  df["month"] = df["date"].dt.month
  # Then XGBoost / LightGBM on these features
  # This approach often beats LSTM for simple series!

━━━ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES ━━━

• Amazon Demand Forecasting: Predicts inventory needs per product per warehouse 7 days ahead. Uses TFT (Temporal Fusion Transformer) at scale — preventing stockouts and overstock.
• Uber Surge Pricing: Predicts ride demand 30 min ahead from historical patterns + events + weather to set surge multipliers.
• ECG / Medical Monitoring: LSTMs detect anomalous heartbeat patterns in real-time ICU monitoring.
• Google Search Trends: ARIMA-based forecasting for ad budget allocation.
• Energy Trading: Predict electricity prices for next 24 hours — LSTM or XGBoost with lag features. Worth millions per day.

━━━ CRITICAL RULE — NO DATA LEAKAGE ━━━

NEVER use future data in training. Always split chronologically:
  # WRONG: random split leaks future into training
  X_train, X_test = train_test_split(df, test_size=0.2, shuffle=True)

  # CORRECT: chronological split
  split_idx = int(len(df) * 0.8)
  train = df.iloc[:split_idx]
  test  = df.iloc[split_idx:]

Use TimeSeriesSplit for cross-validation (sklearn).

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is stationarity and why does ARIMA require it?
A: A stationary series has constant mean, variance, and autocorrelation over time — statistical properties don't drift. ARIMA's AR and MA components assume stationarity. Test with Augmented Dickey-Fuller test. Fix with differencing (subtract previous value) or log transform.

Q: Why can't you use standard k-fold cross-validation for time series?
A: Standard k-fold shuffles data — some training samples would come AFTER test samples in time, creating data leakage. Use TimeSeriesSplit (sklearn) which always trains on past, tests on future.

Q: What is the difference between one-step-ahead and multi-step forecasting?
A: One-step: predict t+1 from t. Multi-step: predict t+1, t+2, ..., t+h. Approaches: recursive (feed predictions back as inputs), direct (train a separate model per horizon), or seq2seq (LSTM/Transformer encoder-decoder).

Q: When would you use Prophet over LSTM?
A: Prophet: business metrics with clear yearly/weekly seasonality, holiday effects, trend breakpoints — and fast iteration is needed. LSTM: complex multivariate patterns, sufficient data (>2 years), and you have GPU training infrastructure.

━━━ WHY WERE RECOMMENDATION SYSTEMS INVENTED? ━━━

BACKDROP:
Amazon (1999) faced a problem: 1 million products, millions of users. How do you show each user the 10 products most likely to interest them?
Manual curation is impossible at scale.
Greg Linden at Amazon invented item-based collaborative filtering (2001) — "customers who bought X also bought Y."
This single innovation contributed ~35% of Amazon's revenue. Netflix's recommendation engine is worth $1B+/year in retention.
The race to personalise the internet had begun.

━━━ THREE MAIN APPROACHES ━━━

APPROACH 1 — COLLABORATIVE FILTERING (CF):
  "Find similar users/items based on past behaviour."
  User-based CF: "Users similar to you also liked X."
  Item-based CF: "Users who liked this also liked Y."

  Matrix Factorisation (SVD):
    Decompose user-item rating matrix (millions × millions) into:
    User latent factors (U: n_users × k)  × Item latent factors (V: k × n_items)
    k = 50-200 latent dimensions (genres, styles, topics — learned automatically)

  from surprise import SVD, Dataset, accuracy
  algo = SVD(n_factors=100)
  algo.fit(trainset)
  pred = algo.predict(user_id="user_123", item_id="item_456")
  # pred.est → estimated rating

APPROACH 2 — CONTENT-BASED FILTERING:
  "Recommend items similar to what this user has liked before — based on item attributes."
  Movie features: genre, director, actors, runtime.
  User profile: weighted average of features of liked movies.
  Cosine similarity between user profile and item features.

  Advantage: no cold-start problem for new items (known attributes immediately).
  Disadvantage: "filter bubble" — only recommends more of the same.

APPROACH 3 — HYBRID (Production Standard):
  Combine both approaches. Netflix, Spotify, YouTube all use hybrid.
  Two-stage architecture:
    Stage 1 — RETRIEVAL (Candidate Generation):
      Fast, approximate — narrow millions of items to ~1000 candidates.
      Use: collaborative filtering, keyword search, ANN vector search.
    Stage 2 — RANKING:
      Slow, precise — rank 1000 candidates to top 10.
      Use: deep learning model (DNN, transformer) with many features.

━━━ DEEP LEARNING FOR RECOMMENDATIONS ━━━

  Wide & Deep (Google Play, 2016):
    Wide: memorisation (linear model on crossed feature pairs)
    Deep: generalisation (deep neural network on embeddings)
    Jointly trained. Standard in industrial recommendation.

  YouTube's DNN (2016):
    Candidate generation: user history + context → embed → dot product with item embeddings → ANN search
    Ranking: deep DNN scoring on ~1000 candidates with dozens of features

  Two-Tower Model:
    User tower: encode user history → user embedding
    Item tower: encode item attributes → item embedding
    Score: cosine similarity between towers
    Train with in-batch negative sampling

━━━ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES ━━━

• Netflix: 80% of watched content comes from recommendations. Saves $1B/year in churn reduction. Uses ensemble of 100+ models per user.
• Spotify Discover Weekly (30M users every Monday): collaborative filtering on listening behaviour → ANN search in song embedding space → personalised 30-song playlist.
• TikTok FYP (For You Page): Reinforcement learning-based rec system — optimises for watch-time-to-completion. So effective it creates addiction concerns.
• Amazon "Frequently bought together": Item-based collaborative filtering on co-purchase graph. Contributed to 35% of Amazon's revenue.
• LinkedIn Jobs: Two-tower model — job seeker embedding + job posting embedding → cosine similarity ranking.

━━━ EVALUATION METRICS ━━━

  Offline:
    Precision@K: of K items shown, what fraction did user actually like?
    Recall@K:    of all items user liked, what fraction appeared in top K?
    NDCG@K:      Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain — rewards relevant items higher in the list
    Mean Average Precision (MAP)

  Online (A/B test):
    CTR (click-through rate)
    Watch time / time-on-site
    Conversion rate
    Revenue per session

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the cold start problem in recommendation systems?
A: New user: no interaction history → cannot compute user similarity or preferences. New item: no interaction history → cannot be recommended via CF. Solutions: ask onboarding questions (explicit preferences), use content-based filtering (item attributes don't need history), popularity-based fallback.

Q: What is the difference between collaborative filtering and content-based filtering?
A: CF uses user-item interaction patterns (who liked what). Content-based uses item attributes (genre, tags). CF can discover unexpected interests; content-based creates filter bubbles but handles new items well. Production systems combine both.

Q: How does Spotify build Discover Weekly?
A: (1) Build user taste profile from listening history embedding. (2) Find similar users using collaborative filtering (ANN in embedding space). (3) Take songs frequently listened by similar users but not by you. (4) Rank by predicted affinity. (5) Add audio diversity constraints. All in a batch pipeline running weekly.

Q: What is negative sampling in two-tower training?
A: For every (user, positive_item) pair, you need negative examples to train. "In-batch negatives": treat the positive items of other users in the same batch as negatives for the current user. Efficient — no extra sampling needed. Used by YouTube, TikTok, Airbnb.

Most ML engineer or data scientist interviews at tech companies follow this structure:

ROUND 1 — CODING (LeetCode-style)
  • 1-2 algorithm problems (arrays, hashmaps, trees, graphs)
  • Python expected. Time: 45-60 min per problem.
  • Target: LeetCode Easy (100%) + Medium (70%)
  • Focus topics: arrays, sliding window, two pointers, BFS/DFS, dynamic programming

ROUND 2 — ML FUNDAMENTALS
  • Explain bias-variance tradeoff.
  • When would you use Random Forest vs XGBoost?
  • How does backpropagation work?
  • What is regularisation and why use it?
  • How do you handle class imbalance?

ROUND 3 — ML SYSTEM DESIGN
  • "Design a recommendation system for Netflix"
  • "Design a real-time fraud detection system"
  • "Design a content moderation system for Twitter"
  Expected: define problem → data → features → model → evaluation → serving → monitoring

ROUND 4 — PROJECT DEEP DIVE
  • Walk through your best project. Be ready for deep technical questions.
  • "Why did you choose XGBoost over Random Forest?"
  • "What was your biggest mistake and how did you fix it?"
  • "What would you do differently now?"

ROUND 5 — BEHAVIOURAL
  • STAR format (Situation, Task, Action, Result)
  • "Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision."
  • "Describe a project that failed."

━━━ ML SYSTEM DESIGN FRAMEWORK ━━━

Use this structure for any ML design question:

  1. CLARIFY: "What is the business objective? What's the scale? Any constraints?"
  2. DATA: "What data do we have? How much? Labels? Freshness?"
  3. METRICS: "How do we measure success? Offline: AUC, NDCG. Online: CTR, conversion."
  4. FEATURES: "What signals are predictive? User history, item attributes, contextual."
  5. MODEL: "Start simple (LR/XGBoost), scale to neural (DNN/Transformer) if needed."
  6. SERVING: "Latency requirement? Batch vs real-time? Caching needed?"
  7. MONITORING: "Data drift, prediction drift, business metrics. Retraining triggers."

━━━ WHY FEATURE SELECTION MATTERS ━━━

BACKDROP — The Problem With More Features:
More features is NOT always better. Every irrelevant or redundant feature adds noise, increases training time, and can cause overfitting.
Curse of dimensionality: in high-dimensional spaces, distance metrics become meaningless and models need exponentially more data.
Feature selection = choose the most informative subset of features before modelling.
Rule in industry: a model with 20 well-chosen features almost always beats a model with 500 raw features.

━━━ THREE FAMILIES OF METHODS ━━━

1. FILTER METHODS (fast, model-agnostic):
  Select features based on statistical relationship with target — independent of any model.
  Fast: computed once, no model training needed.

  from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif, mutual_info_classif, chi2
  import pandas as pd

  # Pearson correlation (linear relationship with numeric target)
  corr = df.corr()["target"].abs().sort_values(ascending=False)
  print(corr.head(20))   # top 20 correlated features

  # F-test (ANOVA): compare variance within vs between classes
  selector = SelectKBest(score_func=f_classif, k=20)
  X_filtered = selector.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)
  selected = X.columns[selector.get_support()].tolist()

  # Mutual Information: captures non-linear relationships
  mi = mutual_info_classif(X_train, y_train)
  mi_series = pd.Series(mi, index=X.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)

  # Chi-squared: for non-negative feature values + classification
  selector_chi2 = SelectKBest(chi2, k=15)

2. WRAPPER METHODS (better, but slow):
  Train a model, evaluate features, repeat. Finds the best subset for THAT model.

  from sklearn.feature_selection import RFE, RFECV
  from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

  # Recursive Feature Elimination
  rfe = RFE(estimator=RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=50), n_features_to_select=20)
  rfe.fit(X_train, y_train)
  selected_rfe = X.columns[rfe.support_].tolist()

  # RFECV: automatically finds optimal number of features via cross-validation
  rfecv = RFECV(estimator=RandomForestClassifier(), step=1, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc")
  rfecv.fit(X_train, y_train)
  print(f"Optimal features: {rfecv.n_features_}")

3. EMBEDDED METHODS (best of both worlds):
  Feature selection happens as part of model training.

  # L1 Regularisation (Lasso): zeroes out weak features during training
  from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV
  lasso = LassoCV(cv=5)
  lasso.fit(X_train, y_train)
  selected_lasso = X.columns[lasso.coef_ != 0].tolist()  # non-zero weights survived

  # Tree Feature Importance (embedded)
  from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
  import numpy as np
  rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100)
  rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
  importances = pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=X.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)
  top20 = importances.head(20).index.tolist()

━━━ BONUS: VARIANCE THRESHOLD (remove constant features) ━━━

  from sklearn.feature_selection import VarianceThreshold
  vt = VarianceThreshold(threshold=0.01)   # drop features with < 1% variance
  X_var = vt.fit_transform(X_train)
  print(f"Dropped {X_train.shape[1] - X_var.shape[1]} near-constant features")

━━━ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES ━━━

• Amazon Pricing Model: Started with 500 product signals (competitor prices, category trends, review scores, inventory). Lasso reduced to 22 predictive features. Same accuracy, 20× faster inference, $2M saved in compute costs annually.
• Genomics (23andMe / bioinformatics): 500,000 genetic variants per patient. Mutual Information + clinical domain knowledge reduces to ~200 SNPs (single-nucleotide polymorphisms) for disease risk prediction.
• Credit Scoring (FICO): 300+ raw variables from credit bureau → 15 hand-selected features. Regulatory requirement: every feature must be explainable to the customer.
• Kaggle Competition Best Practices: Top Kagglers spend 50%+ of time on feature engineering + selection. "The feature game is where competitions are won."
• Fraud Detection (Stripe): Transaction features start at ~1000 signals. Feature importance from XGBoost + domain knowledge reduces to ~80 operational features. Reduces model latency from 200ms to 12ms.

━━━ PRACTICAL WORKFLOW ━━━

  Step 1: Start with ALL features
  Step 2: Remove near-zero variance (VarianceThreshold)
  Step 3: Remove highly correlated pairs (correlation > 0.95, keep one)
  Step 4: Apply filter method (mutual info) for quick ranking
  Step 5: Train Random Forest → check feature importance
  Step 6: RFECV on top 50 features to find optimal count
  Step 7: Retrain final model on selected features only
  Step 8: Verify no performance degradation

━━━ BENEFITS ━━━

• Reduces overfitting: less noise for model to memorise
• Faster training and inference
• Better model interpretability (fewer features = easier to explain)
• Reduces storage and memory requirements
• Helps with regulatory requirements (only keep explainable features)

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the difference between feature selection and dimensionality reduction?
A: Feature selection keeps a subset of ORIGINAL features (interpretable). Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE) creates NEW features as linear/non-linear combinations (not interpretable). Use feature selection when you need to know which inputs matter; PCA when you just need compression.

Q: Why does L1 (Lasso) regularisation perform feature selection but L2 (Ridge) does not?
A: L1 penalty (Σ|w|) creates a diamond-shaped constraint region with corners on axes — the optimal solution often lands exactly at a corner, making some weights exactly zero. L2 penalty (Σw²) creates a spherical constraint — the optimal solution is rarely exactly zero. Lasso zeros out features; Ridge only shrinks them.

Q: What is multicollinearity and how do you detect and fix it?
A: Two or more features are highly correlated — they carry nearly the same information. Causes: inflated coefficient variances in linear models, unstable feature importances. Detect: correlation matrix, VIF (Variance Inflation Factor). Fix: remove one of the correlated pair, use Ridge/PCA to handle redundancy, or use domain knowledge to merge.

Q: When would you use mutual information over Pearson correlation?
A: Pearson correlation only detects linear relationships. Mutual information detects any statistical dependence — linear, non-linear, categorical. Use mutual information for non-numeric features or when you suspect non-linear relationships.

━━━ WHY DBSCAN WAS INVENTED ━━━

BACKDROP — The Problem With K-Means:
K-Means assumes clusters are spherical and roughly equal-sized.
Real-world clusters are often irregular, elongated, or nested.
Martin Ester et al. (1996) invented DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) to cluster geographic data (city neighbourhoods, traffic zones) where shapes are arbitrary and outliers must be explicitly detected.
Key insight: define clusters as dense regions of points separated by sparse regions. Points in sparse regions are "noise" — outliers.

━━━ DBSCAN — HOW IT WORKS ━━━

Two parameters:
  eps (ε): radius to search for neighbours
  min_samples: minimum points within eps to be a "core point"

Point types:
  Core point:    has ≥ min_samples points within eps radius
  Border point:  within eps of a core point, but < min_samples neighbours
  Noise point:   not within eps of any core point → labelled -1 (outlier)

  from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN
  from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
  import numpy as np

  scaler = StandardScaler()
  X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

  db = DBSCAN(eps=0.3, min_samples=10)
  labels = db.fit_predict(X_scaled)

  n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
  n_outliers = np.sum(labels == -1)
  print(f"{n_clusters} clusters, {n_outliers} outliers")

CHOOSING eps:
  Plot k-distance graph: for each point, compute distance to k-th nearest neighbour.
  Sort and plot → find the "elbow" → use that distance as eps.

  from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
  neigh = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=min_samples)
  nbrs = neigh.fit(X_scaled)
  distances, _ = nbrs.kneighbors(X_scaled)
  distances = np.sort(distances[:, -1])
  plt.plot(distances)  # find elbow for eps

━━━ ANOMALY DETECTION METHODS ━━━

ISOLATION FOREST (iForest, 2008):
  Core idea: anomalies are easy to isolate — they need fewer random splits to be separated.
  Build random trees, measure how many splits it takes to isolate each point.
  Anomalies: isolated quickly (shallow trees). Normal points: isolated slowly (deep trees).

  from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
  iso = IsolationForest(
      n_estimators=200,
      contamination=0.05,   # expected proportion of anomalies
      random_state=42
  )
  preds = iso.fit_predict(X)   # +1 = normal, -1 = anomaly
  scores = iso.decision_function(X)   # lower score = more anomalous

LOCAL OUTLIER FACTOR (LOF):
  Compares local density of a point to its neighbours.
  Points in sparse regions relative to neighbours = outliers.
  Better than Isolation Forest for non-uniform density data.

  from sklearn.neighbors import LocalOutlierFactor
  lof = LocalOutlierFactor(n_neighbors=20, contamination=0.05)
  preds = lof.fit_predict(X)   # -1 = outlier

ONE-CLASS SVM:
  Learns the boundary of "normal" data. Points outside boundary = anomalies.
  Best for: high-dimensional data where other methods fail.
  Slower than Isolation Forest for large datasets.

  from sklearn.svm import OneClassSVM
  oc_svm = OneClassSVM(nu=0.05, kernel="rbf", gamma="scale")
  oc_svm.fit(X_train_normal)   # train on ONLY normal samples
  preds = oc_svm.predict(X_test)   # -1 = anomaly

AUTOENCODER-BASED ANOMALY DETECTION (deep learning):
  Train autoencoder on normal data. Reconstruction error = anomaly score.
  Normal data: low reconstruction error (model has learned it).
  Anomaly: high reconstruction error (model never seen this pattern).

  # threshold = mean + 3×std of reconstruction errors on validation normal data
  recon_errors = np.mean(np.square(X_val - autoencoder.predict(X_val)), axis=1)
  threshold = np.percentile(recon_errors, 95)
  anomalies = recon_errors > threshold

• Astronomy (Sloan Digital Sky Survey): DBSCAN identifies galaxy clusters from millions of star positions. Discovered 100K+ unknown galaxy groupings.
• Retail Store Placement: DBSCAN on customer home coordinates to find natural market areas. Informs where to open new stores.

━━━ WHEN TO USE WHICH METHOD ━━━

  Problem                          Best Method
  Geographic/spatial clusters      DBSCAN
  Unknown cluster shape            DBSCAN
  Fast anomaly detection           Isolation Forest
  Labeled normal data available    One-Class SVM or Autoencoder
  Sequence/time series anomaly     LSTM Autoencoder
  High dimensional tabular data    Isolation Forest
  Interpretability required        LOF (gives outlier factor per point)

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the difference between DBSCAN and K-Means?
A: K-Means: requires K upfront, assumes spherical clusters, assigns every point. DBSCAN: no K needed, handles arbitrary shapes, marks outliers as noise (-1). Use DBSCAN when clusters are non-spherical, overlapping, or you want outlier detection built in.

Q: Why does Isolation Forest work well for anomaly detection?
A: Anomalies are rare and different from the majority — they have few neighbours and occupy low-density regions. Random splits isolate them quickly (short path length in tree). Normal points require many splits (long path). The average path length across many trees is the anomaly score.

Q: What is the contamination parameter in Isolation Forest?
A: Expected fraction of anomalies in the dataset. Setting contamination=0.05 means the model will flag the 5% of points with the lowest scores as anomalies. If unknown, use contamination="auto" and set your own threshold on the decision_function scores.

Q: When would an autoencoder be better than Isolation Forest for anomaly detection?
A: When the data has complex sequential or structural patterns (time series, images, sensor streams). Isolation Forest works best on tabular data with independent features. Autoencoders learn the full joint distribution including temporal dependencies.

━━━ WHY WAS AUTOML INVENTED? ━━━

BACKDROP — The Bottleneck in ML:
Building a good ML model requires: choosing an algorithm, preprocessing data, engineering features, tuning hyperparameters, ensembling models.
Each step takes hours of expert time. Most companies don't have ML experts.
AutoML automates the full pipeline — from raw data to a trained model — so non-specialists can use ML, and experts can prototype 10× faster.
Google AutoML (2017), H2O AutoML (2017), AutoSklearn (2015), TPOT (2016) industrialised this.

━━━ WHAT AUTOML AUTOMATES ━━━

  1. Data preprocessing    — imputation, encoding, scaling (automatically)
  2. Feature engineering   — create polynomial, interaction, date features
  3. Algorithm selection   — try 10+ algorithms, select best
  4. Hyperparameter tuning — Bayesian optimisation over huge search spaces
  5. Ensembling            — combine top N models for best performance

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is Bayesian optimisation and how does it improve on grid search?
A: Grid search: try all combinations deterministically (exponential in dimensions). Bayesian optimisation: build a probabilistic model (surrogate) of the objective function. Use it to predict which hyperparameters are most promising → evaluate those → update the model. Each evaluation informs the next. Gets good results with 10× fewer evaluations than grid search.

Q: What is neural architecture search (NAS)?
A: AutoML applied to deep learning architecture design. Automatically searches for the optimal number of layers, layer types, connections, activation functions. EfficientNet and MobileNet were designed partly by NAS. Extremely compute-intensive (hundreds of GPU-days).

Q: Why hasn't AutoML fully replaced ML engineers?
A: AutoML excels at: standard tabular problems, selecting from known algorithm families. It fails at: domain-specific feature engineering, custom architectures (transformers, graph nets), defining appropriate evaluation metrics, debugging surprising results, productionising and monitoring in complex systems.

━━━ WHY SQL IS NON-NEGOTIABLE FOR DATA SCIENTISTS ━━━

BACKDROP:
Every data science interview at FAANG, startups, and enterprise companies includes SQL.
Nearly all production data lives in relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift).
Before you can do any ML, you need to extract, aggregate, and clean data — SQL is how you do it.
SQL was invented in 1974 at IBM (by Donald Chamberlin and Raymond Boyce). It remains THE language for structured data.

━━━ CORE SQL (must know cold) ━━━

BASIC QUERY STRUCTURE:
  SELECT column1, column2, COUNT(*) AS cnt
  FROM table_name
  WHERE column1 > 100
  GROUP BY column1, column2
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
  ORDER BY cnt DESC
  LIMIT 10;

Execution order (NOT written order): FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT

JOINS:
  INNER JOIN: rows matching in both tables.
  LEFT JOIN:  all rows from left, matching from right (NULL where no match).
  RIGHT JOIN: all rows from right (rare — just flip the join).
  FULL OUTER JOIN: all rows from both, NULLs where no match.

  SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count, SUM(o.amount) AS total_spent
  FROM users u
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
  WHERE o.created_at >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY u.id, u.name
  HAVING SUM(o.amount) > 1000
  ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

SUBQUERIES:
  SELECT name, salary
  FROM employees
  WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

CTEs (Common Table Expressions) — cleaner, reusable subqueries:
  WITH monthly_sales AS (
      SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
             SUM(amount) AS revenue
      FROM orders
      GROUP BY 1
  ),
  ranked AS (
      SELECT month, revenue,
             RANK() OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS rnk
      FROM monthly_sales
  )
  SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rnk <= 3;

━━━ WINDOW FUNCTIONS (interview staple) ━━━

Window functions compute values across a sliding "window" of rows — without collapsing rows like GROUP BY does.

  SELECT
    user_id,
    order_date,
    amount,
    SUM(amount)   OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total,
    LAG(amount)   OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_order,
    RANK()        OVER (ORDER BY amount DESC) AS amount_rank,
    ROW_NUMBER()  OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date) AS user_order_num
  FROM orders;

COMMON WINDOW FUNCTIONS:
  ROW_NUMBER()   — sequential number (no ties)
  RANK()         — rank with gaps for ties
  DENSE_RANK()   — rank without gaps
  LAG(col, n)    — value n rows back
  LEAD(col, n)   — value n rows forward
  SUM/AVG/COUNT OVER() — running aggregates

━━━ ANALYTICAL SQL PATTERNS ━━━

FIND TOP N PER GROUP (most common interview question):
  WITH ranked AS (
      SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) AS rn
      FROM products
  )
  SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 3;

COHORT ANALYSIS (retention by signup month):
  WITH cohorts AS (
      SELECT user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', first_purchase) AS cohort_month
      FROM (SELECT user_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_purchase FROM orders GROUP BY 1) sub
  )
  SELECT c.cohort_month,
         DATE_DIFF(DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date), c.cohort_month, MONTH) AS months_since_first,
         COUNT(DISTINCT o.user_id) AS retained_users
  FROM orders o
  JOIN cohorts c USING (user_id)
  GROUP BY 1, 2
  ORDER BY 1, 2;

SELF-JOIN (year-over-year comparison):
  SELECT a.month, a.revenue, b.revenue AS prev_revenue,
         (a.revenue - b.revenue) / b.revenue AS yoy_growth
  FROM monthly_sales a
  JOIN monthly_sales b ON a.month = b.month + INTERVAL '1 year';

FINDING DUPLICATES:
  SELECT email, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

━━━ SQL AT SCALE — BIG DATA SQL ━━━

  Google BigQuery:   petabyte-scale, serverless, pay-per-query (standard SQL)
  AWS Redshift:      columnar data warehouse, fast aggregations on TB+ data
  Snowflake:         cloud-neutral, separated compute/storage, most popular DW 2020-present
  Apache Spark SQL:  SQL on distributed datasets (Python + SQL together)
  Presto/Trino:      Facebook's distributed SQL engine (runs over HDFS, S3, databases)

  # Pandas + SQL together:
  import pandas as pd
  df = pd.read_sql("SELECT * FROM orders WHERE amount > 100", con=engine)

  # Or run SQL on DataFrames directly:
  import duckdb
  result = duckdb.sql("SELECT city, AVG(price) FROM df GROUP BY city").df()

• Uber: SQL on Vertica/Hive to analyse trip patterns, compute surge pricing features, investigate anomalies. A data scientist typically writes 20+ SQL queries per day.

━━━ SQL INTERVIEW QUESTIONS (most common) ━━━

Q: Find the second highest salary from an employees table.
  SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);
  -- Or: SELECT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;

Q: Write a query to find users who placed orders in January but not in February.
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM orders WHERE MONTH(order_date) = 1
  EXCEPT
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM orders WHERE MONTH(order_date) = 2;

Q: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
A: WHERE filters BEFORE grouping (operates on individual rows). HAVING filters AFTER grouping (operates on aggregated values). WHERE cannot reference aggregate functions; HAVING can.

Q: What is the difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()?
A: Both rank rows in order. RANK() skips numbers after ties (1,1,3). DENSE_RANK() does not skip (1,1,2). Use DENSE_RANK() when you want "top N" without gaps.

Q: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
A: DELETE: removes specific rows, can be rolled back, slow (row-by-row). TRUNCATE: removes ALL rows, faster (no row logging), usually can't be rolled back. DROP: removes the entire table structure + data permanently.

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the difference between AutoModel and AutoModelForSequenceClassification?
A: AutoModel loads the base transformer without a task head (just feature extraction). AutoModelForSequenceClassification adds a linear classification head on top. Always use the task-specific class (ForCausalLM, ForTokenClassification, ForQuestionAnswering) — it attaches the right head.

Q: What does tokenizer(return_tensors="pt") do?
A: Converts text to PyTorch tensors ready for the model: input_ids (token integer IDs), attention_mask (1 for real tokens, 0 for padding), and optionally token_type_ids. The model takes these as keyword arguments.

Q: What is the difference between padding and truncation?
A: Truncation: cuts text longer than max_length (prevents CUDA out of memory). Padding: adds [PAD] tokens to shorter sequences so all sequences in a batch have the same length (needed for batch processing). Use both: truncation=True, padding=True.

Q: What is LoRA and why is it used for fine-tuning large LLMs?
A: Low-Rank Adaptation: instead of fine-tuning all 7B parameters, freeze the original model and add small trainable rank-decomposition matrices to attention layers. Only 0.1-1% of parameters are updated. Result: same quality as full fine-tuning but 100× less GPU memory and storage. LLaMA 7B fine-tuned with LoRA fits on a single 16GB GPU.

━━━ WHY AI AGENTS? ━━━

BACKDROP:
Early LLMs were stateless: one question → one answer. No memory, no tools, no multi-step reasoning.
LLMs are powerful reasoners but cannot: browse the internet, execute code, query a database, call APIs.
AI agents solve this: give an LLM tools + memory + a loop. The LLM decides what to do next based on previous results.
"Agents" are the bridge from chatbots to autonomous AI systems that take real actions.
LangChain (2022, Harrison Chase) became the standard framework for building LLM applications and agents.

━━━ RETRIEVAL AUGMENTED GENERATION (RAG) ━━━

Problem: LLMs don't know your proprietary documents, recent events, or specific data.
Solution: retrieve relevant chunks from your knowledge base → inject into context → LLM answers with your data.

  from langchain.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
  from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
  from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
  from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
  from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA

  # 1. Load documents
  loader = PyPDFLoader("company_manual.pdf")
  docs = loader.load()

  # 2. Split into chunks
  splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=500, chunk_overlap=50)
  chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)

  # 3. Embed and store in vector DB
  embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()
  vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(chunks, embeddings, persist_directory="./chroma_db")

  # 4. Create retrieval chain
  qa = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(
      llm=llm,
      retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 4}),
      return_source_documents=True
  )
  result = qa({"query": "What is the leave policy?"})

━━━ AI AGENTS ━━━

An agent uses an LLM as its brain to: reason about a task → pick a tool → observe result → repeat.

AGENT TOOLS:
  from langchain.agents import create_react_agent, AgentExecutor
  from langchain.tools import DuckDuckGoSearchRun, WikipediaQueryRun
  from langchain_experimental.tools import PythonREPLTool

  tools = [
      DuckDuckGoSearchRun(),              # search the internet
      WikipediaQueryRun(),                # query Wikipedia
      PythonREPLTool()                    # execute Python code
  ]

  agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools, prompt)
  executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=tools, verbose=True, max_iterations=5)

  result = executor.invoke({"input": "What is today's price of NVIDIA stock and what is 10% of that?"})
  # Agent: searches for NVIDIA price → gets $950 → executes 950 * 0.1 → returns $95.0

ReAct PATTERN (Reason + Act):
  The agent loop:
    THOUGHT: "I need to find today's NVIDIA price."
    ACTION: DuckDuckGoSearch("NVIDIA stock price today")
    OBSERVATION: "NVIDIA is trading at $950.42"
    THOUGHT: "Now I need to compute 10%."
    ACTION: PythonREPL("950.42 * 0.1")
    OBSERVATION: "95.04"
    FINAL ANSWER: "10% of NVIDIA's current price ($950.42) is $95.04."

━━━ MULTI-AGENT SYSTEMS ━━━

  from langgraph.graph import StateGraph

  # Each agent is a node. Edges define control flow.
  # Agents can: call tools, invoke other agents, loop, branch based on output.
  # Example: Researcher Agent → Writer Agent → Critic Agent → final output

  Used by: AutoGPT, BabyAGI, CrewAI, MetaGPT.

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the difference between a chain and an agent in LangChain?
A: Chain: fixed, predefined sequence of LLM calls and tools. The flow is hardcoded. Agent: the LLM itself decides which tools to use and in what order, based on intermediate observations. Chains are predictable; agents are flexible but less predictable.

Q: What is RAG and why is it better than fine-tuning for knowledge-intensive tasks?
A: RAG retrieves fresh, specific information at query time — always up to date. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into weights at training time — becomes stale, expensive to update, and risks catastrophic forgetting. RAG is also better for proprietary or sensitive data (knowledge stays in your vector DB, not the model).

Q: What is the difference between a vector store and a traditional database for RAG?
A: Traditional DB: exact match on keywords or structured queries. Vector store: semantic similarity search — finds documents with similar MEANING even if no keywords match. E.g., query "heart attack" finds documents containing "myocardial infarction." Uses approximate nearest neighbour (ANN) algorithms.

Q: What is the ReAct agent pattern?
A: Reasoning + Acting interleaved. The LLM first generates a THOUGHT (reasoning about what to do), then an ACTION (tool call), observes the result, and iterates. Combines chain-of-thought reasoning with external tool use. Proposed by Yao et al. (2022).

━━━ WHY MULTI-MODAL AI? ━━━

BACKDROP:
Early AI models were single-modal: a language model processed only text; a vision model processed only images; a speech model processed only audio.
Humans, however, perceive the world through multiple senses simultaneously — we read text on an image, watch a video while hearing audio, and understand the full context.
Multi-modal AI combines two or more modalities (text, image, audio, video) in a single model.
The breakthrough: CLIP (OpenAI, 2021) learned to align images and text in a shared embedding space — zero-shot image classification emerged from this alone.
GPT-4V (2023), Gemini (2023), and Claude 3.x (2024) pushed multi-modal to production scale.

━━━ KEY ARCHITECTURES ━━━

CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining, 2021):
  Two encoders: image encoder (ViT) + text encoder (Transformer)
  Training: 400M (image, alt-text) pairs from the internet
  Objective: image and its caption → similar embedding; random pair → dissimilar

  from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel
  import torch
  from PIL import Image

  model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
  processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

  image = Image.open("cat.jpg")
  texts = ["a photo of a cat", "a photo of a dog", "a photo of a car"]

  inputs = processor(text=texts, images=image, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
  with torch.no_grad():
      outputs = model(**inputs)
  probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)  # probabilities per text
  # → [0.95, 0.04, 0.01]: 95% cat, 4% dog, 1% car — NO labels needed during training!

  WHAT CLIP ENABLES (zero-shot): describe classes in English → classify any image
  Applications: image search, visual content moderation, zero-shot classification

Vision Transformers (ViT):
  Split image into 16×16 pixel patches → flatten → treat as token sequence → pass through Transformer
  No convolutions — pure attention over patch embeddings

  from transformers import ViTForImageClassification, ViTImageProcessor
  processor = ViTImageProcessor.from_pretrained("google/vit-base-patch16-224")
  model = ViTForImageClassification.from_pretrained("google/vit-base-patch16-224")
  inputs = processor(images=image, return_tensors="pt")
  outputs = model(**inputs)
  predicted_class = outputs.logits.argmax(-1).item()

LLaVA (Large Language and Vision Assistant):
  Connect a vision encoder (CLIP) to an LLM (Vicuna/LLaMA) via a projection layer.
  The LLM can now "see" — image patches become visual tokens.

  from transformers import LlavaNextProcessor, LlavaNextForConditionalGeneration
  model = LlavaNextForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("llava-hf/llava-v1.6-mistral-7b-hf")
  # Ask questions about images — the model generates text answers about image content

GPT-4V / Claude 3 / Gemini (Proprietary Multi-modal LLMs):
  These models accept arbitrary interleaved text + images as input.
  Can describe images, extract text from screenshots (OCR), reason about charts/diagrams.
  Example uses: analyse medical scans, read a slide deck, debug a UI screenshot.

━━━ MULTI-MODAL TRAINING TECHNIQUES ━━━

Contrastive Learning: CLIP-style. Pull matched pairs close, push mismatched pairs apart.
Cross-modal Attention: transformer that attends over both text tokens and image patches simultaneously.
Instruction Tuning: show (image, question, answer) triplets — model learns to follow visual instructions.
Causal Modelling (GPT-4V style): treat image patches as tokens → next-token prediction on the combined sequence.

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is contrastive learning and how does CLIP use it?
A: Contrastive learning trains a model to push embeddings of similar pairs together and dissimilar pairs apart. CLIP: for each (image, caption) pair in a batch, maximise similarity between matched pairs and minimise similarity between all other cross-modal pairs. Loss: InfoNCE (a form of cross-entropy on similarity scores).

Q: What is zero-shot classification and why does CLIP enable it?
A: Zero-shot: classify without training on labelled examples of those classes. CLIP's shared text-image embedding space means you can describe a new class in English ("a photo of a golden retriever") and compute its similarity to any image — no training examples needed.

Q: What is the difference between ViT and a CNN for image understanding?
A: CNN: convolutions capture local spatial patterns hierarchically (edges → textures → shapes → objects). ViT: splits image into patches, treats as sequence, self-attention captures global dependencies from the first layer. ViT outperforms CNN at large scale (>1B params, large datasets) but needs more data; CNNs are better with limited data.

Q: How does speech recognition work with Whisper?
A: Audio → log-Mel spectrogram (frequency vs time heatmap) → CNN encoder → Transformer encoder → Transformer decoder generates text tokens (like machine translation from audio to text). Trained on 680K hours of labelled (audio, transcript) pairs.

━━━ WHY CI/CD IS CRITICAL FOR ML ━━━

BACKDROP — The Notebook-to-Production Gap:
A model trained in a Jupyter notebook is a prototype. Getting it to production requires:
• Reproducibility: can anyone on the team retrain with the same result?
• Automation: can the entire pipeline (data → train → evaluate → deploy) run without human intervention?
• Safety: if code changes break model performance, the system should catch it before users are affected.
ML CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) automates this entire lifecycle.
Without it: teams spend 70% of time on manual deployment, models are impossible to audit, bugs reach production.

━━━ DATA VERSIONING WITH DVC ━━━

DVC (Data Version Control): Git for data files and ML models.
Problem: datasets are GB/TB — you cannot put them in Git.
Solution: DVC stores the data in cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure) and tracks a tiny pointer file in Git.

  pip install dvc dvc-s3

  dvc init
  dvc add data/train.csv              # creates data/train.csv.dvc pointer
  git add data/train.csv.dvc .dvc/   # commit the pointer to git
  git commit -m "add training data v1"

  dvc remote add -d myremote s3://my-bucket/dvc-storage
  dvc push                             # upload actual data to S3

  # Reproduce the full ML pipeline
  dvc run -n train \
      -d data/train.csv -d src/train.py \
      -o models/model.pkl \
      python src/train.py

  dvc repro                            # run only stages whose inputs changed
  dvc dag                              # visualise the pipeline DAG

━━━ GITHUB ACTIONS FOR ML ━━━

Automated testing and deployment triggered on every git push or PR.

  # .github/workflows/ml-pipeline.yml
  name: ML CI/CD Pipeline

  on:
    push:
      branches: [main]
    pull_request:
      branches: [main]

  jobs:
    test-and-train:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - uses: actions/checkout@v3
        - uses: actions/setup-python@v4
          with: { python-version: '3.11' }

        - name: Install dependencies
          run: pip install -r requirements.txt

        - name: Run unit tests
          run: pytest tests/ -v

        - name: Run data validation
          run: python src/validate_data.py  # check schema, distributions

        - name: Train model
          run: python src/train.py

        - name: Evaluate model
          run: python src/evaluate.py     # fails if accuracy < threshold

        - name: Deploy to staging
          if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
          run: |
            docker build -t mymodel:${{ github.sha }} .
            docker push registry/mymodel:${{ github.sha }}
            kubectl set image deployment/ml-api container=registry/mymodel:${{ github.sha }}

━━━ MODEL TESTING (beyond unit tests) ━━━

  # 1. Data schema tests (Great Expectations)
  import great_expectations as ge
  df = ge.read_csv("data/train.csv")
  df.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("label")
  df.expect_column_values_to_be_between("age", min_value=0, max_value=120)
  df.expect_column_to_exist("user_id")

  # 2. Model performance threshold tests
  def test_model_performance():
      auc = evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test)["auc"]
      assert auc >= 0.85, f"Model AUC {auc:.3f} below threshold 0.85"

  # 3. Slice-based evaluation (fairness testing)
  for age_group in ["18-30", "31-50", "51+"]:
      subset = test_data[test_data.age_group == age_group]
      recall = recall_score(subset.y_true, model.predict(subset.X))
      assert recall >= 0.7, f"Recall for {age_group} = {recall:.3f} below 0.70"

  # 4. Latency test
  import time
  start = time.perf_counter()
  _ = model.predict(X_test[:100])
  latency = (time.perf_counter() - start) / 100 * 1000
  assert latency < 50, f"Inference too slow: {latency:.1f}ms > 50ms"

━━━ AUTOMATED RETRAINING PIPELINES ━━━

  TRIGGER-BASED RETRAINING:
    Trigger retraining when:
    • New data volume threshold exceeded (e.g., 10K new labelled examples)
    • Data drift detected (PSI > 0.2 on key features)
    • Model performance drops below threshold on production metrics
    • Weekly/monthly scheduled retraining (calendar-based)

  TOOLS:
    Apache Airflow:    DAG-based workflow scheduler (industry standard, complex setup)
    Prefect:           Modern Python-native scheduler (easier than Airflow)
    AWS Step Functions:Serverless pipeline orchestration
    Kubeflow Pipelines:ML pipelines on Kubernetes

━━━ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES ━━━

• Netflix: Every recommendation model retrained weekly via automated Airflow pipeline. Triggered by: new viewing data accumulated. CI: unit tests + A/B performance gate + canary deployment to 1% traffic before full rollout.
• Uber: Real-time feature engineering pipeline + model training runs on Spark every hour. Models must pass accuracy + latency tests before being promoted to production traffic.
• Airbnb: Automated pipeline using DVC + GitHub Actions. PRs trigger: data validation → model training → performance evaluation. Failed gate = PR blocked automatically.
• Google Search: Continuous model updates — some components retrain multiple times per day. CI/CD ensures backward compatibility and performance regression detection.

━━━ INTERVIEW QUESTIONS ━━━

Q: What is the difference between CI and CD in ML?
A: CI (Continuous Integration): automatically test every code change — data validation, unit tests, model performance gates. Catches regressions early. CD (Continuous Deployment): automatically deploy models that pass CI to staging/production without manual intervention. CD requires confidence in your testing.

Q: What is a model registry and why is it important in MLOps?
A: Central storage for trained model artifacts with versioning, metadata (training date, dataset version, metrics), and stage transitions (Staging → Production → Archived). MLflow Model Registry is the standard. Enables rollback: if production model degrades, you can instantly revert to the previous registered version.

Q: What is canary deployment in ML?
A: Gradually route traffic to a new model version while monitoring performance. Start at 1% → 5% → 25% → 100%. If metrics degrade at any step, automatically roll back. Safer than instant full deployment.

Q: What is data validation and why is it the first step in any ML pipeline?
A: Check that incoming data matches expected schema, ranges, and distributions before training or serving. Bad data can silently corrupt models — "garbage in, garbage out." Great Expectations is the standard library. Catches: missing columns, type changes, distribution shifts, null spikes.
